course_description:
This course builds on CHEE3007 Process Modelling and Dynamics and considers a range of techniques required to model complex and unfamiliar processes. Students use advanced features of relevant modelling and programming software. Large scale experimental data from processes or experiments are used to build 'data driven' models for a range of applications. Following this, students integrate data-driven and mechanistic models to develop and apply hybrid modelling to model industrial or experimental processes. The projects are derived from current industry applications.

Modern chemical engineering is increasingly model-centric , with design and operational decision‑making guided by a wide range of models with differing structures, assumptions, and levels of complexity. This course examines several types of models, exploring their formulation, nature, solution and simulation. Students begin by analysing complex, dynamic industrial data and then use this information to build or validate empirical, mechanistic, or grey‑box models. Approaches such as system identification and principal component analysis are emphasised throughout. Learning is driven by authentic industry‑based projects , allowing students to develop modelling capability, technical judgement, and decision‑making skills aligned with modern professional practice.

Python and MATLAB/Simulink are used throughout the course for data analysis, model development, and simulation.

UQ Public Timetable
.
course_aims: To extend the knowledge and skills acquired from CHEE3007 Process Modelling and Dynamics and CHEE4009 Transport Phenomena. The goal is to address advanced modelling techniques, applied to industrial designs and operation, which can have a significant impact on decision making. The course will address the underlying characteristics of process systems as represented in very large dynamic data sets to build insights into the underlying system dynamics. It will provide skills in evaluating the quality of the data and its use in building empirical models. Data-driven, mechanistic and hybrid modelling ﾠwill be investigated. Practical use of modelling and solution environments will be emphasized, along with the fundamentals of good modelling practice. The fundamental nature of multiscale models will be examined. Students will develop their critical thinking and communication skills, such that they can ask essential questions in model development and application, such as What is the purpose of the model? How can the model be used for decision making? What are the key limitations of the model? What reliability/confidence/accuracy/uncertainty is required or expected from a specific model? What additional information would improve your predictions? What insights can you glean from the model?

learning_outcomes.description: Clean, tidy, explore, visualize, analyze and develop models to provide insight into large industrial datasets
Explain the opportunities and challenges for advanced modelling approaches in a wide range of system applications, across research, design and operations
Evaluate, select and apply appropriate modelling methodologies for particular outcomes, including both mechanistic and empirical models, and hybrid systems

Critically assess the validity of the assumptions underpinning your model, using data, logic and mechanistic knowledge; assess whether the model is fit for purpose; evaluate the reliability of model output; identify what information is required to improve confidence in model predictions
Apply critical thinking skills to analyze, synthesize and effectively communicate technical information
Demonstrate professional conduct in engaging with industry, including an appreciation of sustainability
Improve teamwork skills through practice, reflection, feedback and change

assessment_details.title: Non-Disclosure Agreement
assessment_details.task_description: This course uses real industrial data in project work and assessment. In order to access this Confidential Information, students are required to sign a Non-Disclosure Agreement, in which they agree not to disclose confidential information to any external party, unless required to do so by law. At the end of the course, students are required to permanently erase or physically destroy (by shredding) all Confidential Information received under the Non-Disclosure Agreement. The Confidential Information can be described as Process Flow Sheets, Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams, plant operating manuals and process descriptions, plant data and standard operating procedures, regardless of whether such information is designated as “Confidential Information” at the time of its disclosure. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Signed and returned to the course coordinator via the Blackboard submission portal.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessment_details.title: Individual exercises
assessment_details.task_description: Individual exercise 1 Due 12 Mar 17:00 Weight: 10% Performance on nominated individual case studies and set tasks, from Part A content. Individual exercise 2 Due 1 May 15:00 Weight: 10% Performance on nominated individual case studies and set tasks from Part B content. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of the assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission, independent of AI and MT tools. assessment_details.title: Project Presentation and Project Part A
assessment_details.due_date: 27/03/2026 2:00 pm 2/04/2026 5:00 pm Single combined submission for CHEE7111 and CHEE7113
assessment_details.task_description: Project presentation Part A Due 27/03 14:00 Weight: 5% Presentation to students, staff and industry personnel. A single, combined presentation will be completed for both the CHEE7111 and CHEE7113 components of the project. Students will be required to mark and respond to presentations by other groups, as well as present their own work. Each group will upload a presentation to Blackboard prior to the scheduled presentation time, then either play a recording of their presentation or present live, and then respond live to questions and comments from teaching staff, classmates and industry partner. Exact timing will depend on the industry partner's availability; the estimated due date is the class time on that date. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in preparing the slides of their oral presentation - see further details about using AI or MT below. However, the oral presentation involving questions and answers is to be delivered in-person and the use of generative AI or MT tools to deliver the oral presentation will not be permitted. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Project report Part A Due 02/04 17:00 Weight: 20% A team-based project focused on a process system of a complex nature. It will involve both a written submission and a team-based presentation to the client(s). The single project report will cover Part A projects from both CHEE7111 and CHEE7113. Submissions will be through Blackboard. A Turnitin link will be made available before the due date. AI and MT may support students in completing this assessment task - see further details about using AI or MT below. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Further Details about Using AI or MT The project presentation and project report involves industry‑supplied documents and/or data. Students must not upload, input, summarise, paraphrase, or otherwise disclose any industry‑supplied information into generative AI or external MT tools under any circumstances. This includes raw data, processed data, figures, tables, text from industry documents, screenshots, file names, or any content that could reveal confidential or proprietary information. Only de‑identified, student‑generated content may be used with AI or MT tools where this is permitted. Breaching this requirement may compromise industry confidentiality and may constitute student misconduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.title: Exam During Exam Period (School) - Individual Oral defence
assessment_details.task_description: This is an oral assessment: you will speak on a topic of your choosing for 3 minutes, then defend your comments on that topic and respond to other questions related to the course. This assessment task evaluates students' abilities, skills and knowledge without the aid of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT). Students are advised that the use of AI or MT technologies to develop responses is strictly prohibited and may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_resources.library_resources: Find the required and recommended resources for this course on the UQ Library website.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 6 (23 Feb - 05 Apr)
learning_activities.topic: Part A: Data analysis and data driven modelling All CHEE7111 and CHEE7113 timetabled classes in Week 1 will be devoted to CHEE7111 , and in Week 2, all CHEE7111 and CHEE7113 classes will be devoted to CHEE7113 . From Week 3 onward , both CHEE7111 and CHEE7113 classes will run each week. Students will be introduced to key aspects of data analysis and data‑driven modelling during workshop sessions, and will apply these skills through workshops and mentoring activities. Python will be used throughout part A for data processing, analysis, visualisation, and the development of data‑driven models. Content includes systems modelling and data processing, multivariate analysis, time‑series analysis, and the construction, application, and interpretation of data‑driven models. Guest lectures and engagement with the industry partner will occur in parallel with the workshop and mentoring program. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 7 To Week 13 (13 Apr - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: Part B: Modelling application to control All CHEE7111 and CHEE7113 timetabled classes in Week 7 will be devoted to CHEE7111 , and in Week 8 all CHEE7111 and CHEE7113 classes will be devoted to CHEE7113 . From Week 9 onward , both CHEE7111 and CHEE7113 classes will run each week. Students will be introduced to the fundamental principles of good modelling practice in both empirical and mechanistic modelling during workshop sessions, and will apply these skills through workshops and mentoring activities. Python and MATLAB/Simulink will be used throughout part B for model calibration, assessment, visualisation, and implementation in process control applications. Content includes best‑practice approaches to model development, model evaluation, communicating results, and applying models to advanced control strategies. Guest lectures and engagement with the industry partner will occur in parallel with the workshop and mentoring program, supporting students as they complete individual assessment tasks and an applied industry project delivered in coordination with CHEE7113. Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure AI for Assessment Guide Recording of Teaching Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library . School guidelines Your school has additional guidelines you'll need to follow for this course: Safety Induction for Practicals Course guidelines Anyone undertaking courses with a practical component must complete the UQ Undergraduate Student Laboratory Safety Induction and pass the associated assessment. Specific instructions, usage guidelines and rules for each of the undergraduate laboratories will be delivered as part of each course. In some cases, students may be required to attend a specific face-to-face laboratory induction/training session. Learning activities
